mnemonics.title: P-P-P Memory Aid
mnemonics.content: Remember Access Specifiers: Public - Everyone can access; Protected - Kids (derived classes) can access; Private - Only Parents (class itself) can access.

mnemonics.title: Friend Functions Rules
mnemonics.content: F.R.I.E.N.D - F: Functions not members; R: Rights to private data; I: Inheritance - not inherited; E: Explicit declaration needed; N: Not transitive; D: Declared in class body.

mnemonics.title: Encapsulation Definition
mnemonics.content: E.N.C.A.P.S.U.L.A.T.I.O.N - External interfaces, Nobody sees data, Controlled access, Abstraction principle, Security, Underlying implementation hidden, Levels of access, Thoughtful design, Integrity, Object-centric, No direct manipulation

acronyms.usage: Programming paradigm based on objects containing data and code
Basic model showing how encapsulated objects interact

visualTips.visualization: Think of access levels like a house: public (front door - everyone enters), protected (bedroom - family only), private (safe - owner only). Draw three nested boxes with labels.
Draw two circles (classes) with a dashed line connecting them for friendship, solid line for inheritance. The dashed line shows special access bridge.
Draw a capsule (pill shape) with 'data' inside and 'methods' on the outer surface, with small openings (interfaces) connecting outside.

analogies.analogy: Like giving your house key to a specific friend - they can access your private space even though they're not family (not a member).
Think of a bank: public counter (general info), protected vault (staff only), private records (managers only).
Like a car - you interact through steering/wheels (public interface), but engine parts are hidden (private) for safety and to prevent misuse.
